topic: Area of Study 1: Instrumental Music of Haydn, Mozart and Beethoven
subject: music

items.q: Is the recapitulation an exact repeat of the exposition?
items.correct: No - the second subject returns in the tonic rather than the dominant, resolving the movement's key tension.
items.misconception: The recapitulation repeats the exposition note for note to round off the movement.
items.random_wrong: The recapitulation is always played twice as fast.
items.why: The tonal adjustment is the whole point of sonata form: bringing both subjects home to the tonic is what gives the recapitulation its sense of resolution.

items.q: Is a symphony a single piece of music?
items.correct: No - a Classical symphony is a multi-movement work, normally in four movements with contrasting tempos and characters.
items.misconception: A symphony is one long continuous piece without breaks.
items.random_wrong: A symphony is any piece that features a violin solo.
items.why: Students meet single-movement excerpts and assume that is the whole work; understanding the four-movement plan is essential for discussing Haydn, Mozart and Beethoven.

items.q: What actually happens in the development section?
items.correct: Material from the exposition is transformed through modulation, fragmentation and sequence, creating instability before the recapitulation.
items.misconception: The development is simply where the music gets louder and more instruments join in.
items.random_wrong: The development introduces an entirely unrelated set of themes.
items.why: Development is a thematic and tonal process, not a dynamic one; recognising transformed fragments of the subjects is a core analytical skill in this repertoire.

items.q: Which instruments make up a string quartet?
items.misconception: Violin, viola, cello and double bass
items.why: Correct answer: Two violins, viola and cello. “Violin, viola, cello and double bass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In a major-key sonata-form exposition, the second subject usually appears in which key?
items.why: Correct answer: The dominant. “The subdominant” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A broken-chord accompaniment pattern in the left hand
items.misconception: A sustained pedal note in the cellos
items.random_wrong: A fugue subject in the bass
items.why: It's A broken-chord accompaniment pattern in the left hand. “A sustained pedal note in the cellos” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer replaced the minuet with the scherzo and greatly expanded the coda?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Beethoven. “Haydn” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many 'London' symphonies did Haydn compose for his visits to England?
items.why: The real figure is 12 symphonies. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
